sections.title: Standard Transaction Process
sections.contentHtml:
The standard Mexican property purchase follows a defined sequence involving the notario público as the central legal authority. Unlike in the U.S., the notario in Mexico is a government-appointed official — not a private attorney — who has binding responsibility for the legality and tax compliance of the transaction.

StepAction
PartyKey Compliance Point
1Obtain RFC (fiscal ID)
BuyerRequired before any contract or bank account. Foreign buyers obtain at SAT office or via power of attorney.
2Property search — portals or regional MLS
BuyerVerify agent's AMPI membership and NOM-247 contract registration if applicable. Check listing is not ejido land.
3Engage a notario público and independent lawyer
BuyerNotario conducts due diligence: title search, lien check, tax status, seller authority. Hire independent legal counsel (separate from notario and seller's agent).
4Restricted zone check
Buyer / LawyerConfirm property coordinates with SRE if near coast or border. Determine if fideicomiso is required.
5SRE fideicomiso permit (if in restricted zone)
Buyer / BankApply through Mexican bank. SRE approval typically within 5 business days.
6Sign purchase agreement / promissory contract
Buyer + SellerDeposit paid (typically 10–30%). Document must comply with NOM-247 contract requirements.
7AML / LFPIORPI due diligence
All partiesKYC required. Proof of funds source. Beneficial owner identification. Cash restrictions apply.
8Pay ISAI (acquisition tax)
BuyerRate varies 2–5% by state. Paid to notario before or at closing.
9Closing — sign Escritura Pública before notario
Buyer + Seller + NotarioNotario verifies all documents, taxes paid. Deed executed. Balance paid (typically by wire transfer).
10Register in Public Property Registry (RPPyC)
Buyer / NotarioRegistration completes ownership transfer. Notario typically handles this.
11Predial (property tax) obligations begin
Buyer (new owner)Annual predial billed by municipality. Early payment (Jan–Feb) = up to 25% discount.

The Notario Público — Central Role

The notario público (Notary Public) in Mexico is a government-appointed professional with legal training who serves simultaneously as a legal authority and a government agent. The notario's role is far broader than in common-law countries: they verify title, confirm all taxes are paid, validate the seller's authority, assess AML compliance, and officially register the deed. All real estate transactions in Mexico must be executed before a notario.

* The buyer has the right under NOM-247 to select the notario who will formalize the purchase.

* Notario fees: typically 0.75–1.5% of property value. Fees are regulated by state aranceles (fee schedules).

* Notario cannot proceed without proof of ISAI payment, clear title, and satisfied LFPIORPI requirements.

LFPIORPI NOTARIO THRESHOLD — July 2025 Reform (Previously Missing): The July 2025 reform reduced the notario's LFPIORPI reporting threshold for real property transfers from 16,000 UMAs to 8,000 UMAs (approximately MXN $904,000 at 2025 UMA values). Any property transfer at or above this value now triggers mandatory notario due diligence and SAT reporting. Platforms displaying property values should flag listings at or above this threshold in any compliance workflow or transaction management tool.
